coredata.dc:title: The improved water oxidation at neutral pH using field bindweed and NiCo- layered double hydroxides
coredata.dc:creator: Jafari Foruzin, Leila
Rezvani, Zolfaghar
coredata.dc:description: High-efficiency and environmental electro-catalyst are prepared for the water oxidation. Here, we report nitrogen doping‑carbon quantum dot (N-CQD) that is prepared from field bindweed using the hydrothermal method. Also, NiCo-layered double hydroxide (LDH) was prepared using a simple method. The prepared samples were characterized using several techniques that confirmed the successful preparation of N-CQD and NiCo-LDH. Also, the effect of molar ratios of Ni: Co on the morphology of NiCo-LDH were studied by the Field Emission-Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM). The obtained images obviously reveal that apply several molar ratios of Ni and Co can affect the morphology and optimized molar ratio Ni: Co was 1: 1. Then, we use N-CQDs combination with the optimized NiCo-LDH for water oxidation. The resulting NiCo-LDH/N-CQD shows improved electro-catalytic activity in comparison with pure NiCo-LDH (with an onset-potential of 1.55 V (vs. RHE) and overpotential about ~ 325 mV in neutral media) for oxygen evolution. The enhanced water oxidation activity is attributed to the association of the NiCo-LDH with N-CQDs. N-CQDs which supply rapid electron transfer, supreme conductivity, and electron reservoir characters that are caused to the rapid electron transfers from N-CQDs to NiCo-LDH on the surface and consequently, could further increase the electro-catalytic reaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N‑carbon quantum dots
NiCo-LDH
Water oxidation
Field bindweed

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the working electrode and electrochemical investigations
Electrocatalytic activity of the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD for the water oxidation reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) NiCo-LDH [Ni: Co = (1: 1), (2: 1), (3:1) and (4: 1)] and (b) Ni(OH)2, Co(OH)2, and N-CQD.
FT-IR spectra of (a) NiCo-LDH (Ni: Co = 1: 1), (b) NiCo-LDH (Ni: Co = 2: 1), (c) NiCo-LDH (Ni: Co = 3: 1), (d) NiCo-LDH (Ni: Co = 4: 1), and (e) N-CQD.
FE-SEM micro and nano-graphs of NiCo-LDH with molar ratio of Ni: Co (a), (b) 1: 1; (c), (d) 1: 2; e, (f) 1: 3; and (g), (h) 1: 4.
EDAX analysis of (a) NiCo-LDH (Ni: Co = 1: 1) and (b) N-CQD.
(a) absorption and (b) emission spectra of N-CQD.

(a) LSV curves of the Ni(OH)2, Co(OH)2 and NiCo-LDH in different molar ratios (1: 1 to 1: 4), (b) the overpotential bar graph of Ni(OH)2, Co(OH)2 and NiCo-LDH in different ratios of Ni: Co, (c) LSV curves of several weight ratios of NiCo-LDH: N-CQD and (d) the overpotential bar graph of several weight ratios of NiCo-LDH: N-CQD.
Nyquist plots of the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD (1: 0.25) composite, N-CQD and NiCo-LDH. The inset presents the impedance in the high frequency region of the two samples.
(a) Chronoamperometric curve of NiCo-LDH/N-CQD at 1.75 V vs. RHE for 24 h, and (b) the LSV curves of the same electro-catalyst after 12 h water oxidation and after 500 cycles.
Tafel plots of NiCo-LDH (1: 1) and optimized NiCo-LDH/N-CQD.
Schematic representation of preparation of N-CQD from stems and leaves of field bindweed

head.ce:title: The improved water oxidation at neutral pH using field bindweed and NiCo- layered double hydroxides

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, P.O. Box 53714-161, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, P.O. Box 53714-161, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-efficiency and environmental electro-catalyst are prepared for the water oxidation. Here, we report nitrogen doping‑carbon quantum dot (N-CQD) that is prepared from field bindweed using the hydrothermal method. Also, NiCo-layered double hydroxide (LDH) was prepared using a simple method. The prepared samples were characterized using several techniques that confirmed the successful preparation of N-CQD and NiCo-LDH. Also, the effect of molar ratios of Ni: Co on the morphology of NiCo-LDH were studied by the Field Emission-Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM). The obtained images obviously reveal that apply several molar ratios of Ni and Co can affect the morphology and optimized molar ratio Ni: Co was 1: 1. Then, we use N-CQDs combination with the optimized NiCo-LDH for water oxidation. The resulting NiCo-LDH/N-CQD shows improved electro-catalytic activity in comparison with pure NiCo-LDH (with an onset-potential of 1.55 V (vs. RHE) and overpotential about ~ 325 mV in neutral media) for oxygen evolution. The enhanced water oxidation activity is attributed to the association of the NiCo-LDH with N-CQDs. N-CQDs which supply rapid electron transfer, supreme conductivity, and electron reservoir characters that are caused to the rapid electron transfers from N-CQDs to NiCo-LDH on the surface and consequently, could further increase the electro-catalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: The water oxidation in order to evaluation of oxygen is a possible method for the production of energy (Bonchio et al., 2019; Li et al., 2016; Pan et al., 2020; Sinha et al., 2020). But, the oxygen evolution is a multistep process that caused to slow proton-coupled electron transfer. Therefore the water oxidation needs a potential higher than that thermodynamic potential (1.23 V) (Hosseini and Shahrokhian, 2019; Zhu et al., 2018). Rarely metal oxides such as IrO2 and RuO2 are active electro-catalysts for water oxidation. However, the high cost of ruthenium and iridium makes them impractical to apply at a large scale water oxidation (Cherevko et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2019a). So, the use of catalysts that have improving efficiency and also are expensive offers as an alternative for large-scale water oxidation.

A class of layered materials is layered double hydroxides (LDH) materials. These materials due to chemical versatility and flexible structure have been considered as electro-catalyst, a chemical sensors, and catalysis (Li et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018; Zhan et al., 2018). Among of transition-metal- electrocatalysts, the cobalt- and nickel-based materials, are candidates for water oxidation due to their low cost, multiple valence states, high stability at water oxidation, and environmentally friendly (Jiang et al., 2015; Song and Hu, 2014). LDH samples have an open and layered structure that makes easy diffusion of water (reactant) and transfer process of the proton-coupled electron at oxygen evaluation but the poor electrical conductivity limits their's electrocatalytic application. Although carbon-based materials such as graphene and carbon nano tube have been applied in composite form with LDH for water oxidation applications (Chen et al., 2016; Jia et al., 2016; Ning et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2019b). The synthesis of nano carbon materials using simple methods and expensive materials remains a weakness. For example, Liu et al. reported the intermixture of a NiFe-LDH with graphene can improve the water oxidation reaction with an overpotential of ~250 mV in 0.1 M KOH (Xia et al., 2016) but the graphene which they applied needs reduced step via a modified Hummers method.

Nitrogen doped-Carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), are a family of nano carbon materials (NCMs) that nitrogen and other functional groups put on the surface of the sample. These materials have been attracted due to unparalleled chemical and physical properties (Namdari et al., 2017). The behaviors such as significant small size and photoluminescence properties and considerable conductivity make them much suitable for improving the electrochemical reactions (Liu et al., 2017). Lastly, carbon-based materials containing nitrogen atoms have been fallowed as a suitable candidate for electrochemical applications (Geng et al., 2016; Kim et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). Many researchers have been studied on the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon materials (N-CMs) and their applications as photo-catalyst in water oxidation (Aghamali et al., 2018; Hsu et al., 2018). numerous N-CMs have been prepared with various methods such as the reaction of carbon-based materials and ammonia under high temperature (Gu et al., 2018), carbonization of nitrogen-based materials at high temperature (Chen et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2010), or chemical vapor deposition method (Jin et al., 2011). Although, the methods that were mentioned above have limits such as difficult synthesis conditions, time-consuming, and high cost. These limits drawback their large scale applications. So, applying simple, low-cost, and easy methods for the preparation of nano carbon-based materials are considerably requested. Also, many researchers have been focused on expending green methods for the synthesis of N-CMs and nano-particles from natural plants (Iravani, 2011; Mhamane et al., 2011; Shankar et al., 2004).

Here, we report producing N-CQD from weed (field bindweed). In this study, we extend a low-cost and easy method toward prepared N-CQD from field bindweed by hydrothermal technology. Then NiCo-LDH prepared with the easy and hydrothermal method. After the characterization of the obtained materials, the mixture of NiCo-LDH and N-CQD was used as an efficient electro-catalyst for water oxidation in neutral electrolytes. We conclude that the optimized mixture of N-CQD and NiCo-LDH is a better electro-catalyst than either the pure NiCo-LDH or pristine N-CQD. The resulting N-CQD/NiCo-LDH show excellent oxygen evaluation activity with considerable high current density and the overpotential about 325 mV in pH = 7. This is the first attempt on the preparation of N-CQD using the weed. Also, N-CQD based materials as an improved electro-catalyst for the oxygen evaluation reaction have been never reported previously.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), and Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), were bought from Sigma-Aldrich company. Field bindweed obtained from the garden. All pristine materials were used without purification.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the obtained materials was investigated by a D8 advanced (Bruker AXS) diffractometer (40 kV, 35 mA) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54187 Å). The morphology of samples were investigated by the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) by model Mira 3-XMU and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) model JEM-2100F. Shimadzu FT-IR model Prestige 21 spectrometer was applied in order to record FT-IR spectra using KBr method. Also, the photoluminescence (PL) curve was recorded on a FP–6200 spectrofluorometer. UV–Vis absorption spectrum was obtained using a T80 UV–Vis spectrophotometer model PG Instrument Ltd.

The products were prepared by the hydrothermal method. Firstly, an aqueous solution containing nitrate of the nickel and cobalt metal cations with different molar ratios (1: 1, 2: 1, 3: 1 and 4: 1 mmol) were mixed and then the solution was hydrolyzed using 5 mmol urea for 30 min. The obtained colloid was transferred into a Teflon and treated under the hydrothermal condition at 90 °C and 9 h. The obtained precipitate was filtered, washed twice time with water, and ethanol. Then the samples were dried at 40 °C.

10 g field bindweed (stems and leaves) and 40 mL distillation water transfer into autoclave and treatment at 180 °C for 6 h. Cellulose and amino acids were as carbon and nitrogen sources at field bindweed. Under hydrothermal process carbonization of the cellulose and amino acids can be occurred. After carbonization, the yellow solution was filtered and dried using freeze drying method. The obtained N-CQDs contain of amino (−NH2), hydroxide (-OH), and carboxyl (-COOH) groups (as presented at Scheme 1 .).

0.01 g prepared NiCo-LDH was dispersed at 3 mL water, and the water solution contains 0.25, 5, 10 and 20 mg N-CQD were added (weight ration LDH: CQD were 1: 0.25, 1: 0.5, 1: 1 and 1: 2). Then the obtained colloid solution was sonicated for 30 min at 40 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the working electrode and electrochemical investigations
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical investigations were carried out in a three-electrode system using a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Hg/Hg2Cl2 and platinum wire were applied as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The surface of GCE was polished using a polishing paper and alumina paste to obtain a mirror-like surface, then ultra-sonicated in a mixture water and acetone solution with volume ration 1: 1. 10 mg electro-catalyst. Then the mixture of 3 mL NiCo-LDH/N-CQD colloid solutions and in 1 μL Nafion/water solution (0.5 wt%, Alfa Aesar) were dispersed by ultra-sonication method. Then 5 μL of the prepared homogeneous solution was drop-casted onto the GCE. The modified GCE was dried at room temperature. The electrochemical measurements were performed by using an AUTOLAB PGSTAT-100 potentiostat/galvanostat. All the electrochemical tests of electro-catalysts were carried out in 0.1 M sodium phosphate solution (pH = 7). The potential, measured against a Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode, was converted to the potential versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to E (vs. RHE) = E (vs. Hg/Hg2Cl2) + 0.242 + (0.059 × pH).

X-ray diffraction patterns of NiCo-LDH samples at different molar ratios, Co(OH)2, (Ni(OH)2, and N-CQD were carried out for the study phase and structure of samples. The XRD characterization of nickel‑cobalt layered double hydroxide synthesized by the hydrothermal method with several ratios [(1: 1), (2: 1), (3: 1) and (4: 1)] are illustrated in Fig. 1(a). Peaks appearing at the diffraction angle (2θ) of 11.5°, 23.9°, 34.4°, and 60.50 corresponding to the (003), (006), (012), and (110) plates, respectively that confirm the synthesis of layered like structure (JCPDS 33–0429) (Gilanizadeh and Zeynizadeh, 2018; Wang et al., 2017). In these patterns, the interlayer separation was calculated about 0.70 nm that indicates present carbonate ions at interlayer space. As shown at Fig. 1(b), the diffraction pattern for Ni(OH)2 are present the peaks at 12.2°, 23.9°, 33.4°, 34.9° and 59.9°, which can be attributed to the planes of (001), (002), (110), (111), and (300) in the α-Ni(OH)2 (JCPDS 22–0444). At the pattern of Co(OH)2, four characteristic peaks located at 11.1°, 22.2°, 38. 1°, and 59.0° that ascribed to (003),(006), (015), and (110) crystalline planes of α-Co(OH)2 (Wang et al., 2011). As also, Fig. 1(b) presents the diffraction pattern of N-CQD. In this pattern, the plates (002), (100), (102) and (103) appear at the diffraction angles of 28°, 41°, 50° and 590, respectively, confirming the synthesis of carbon quantum dot (Hsu et al., 2012). In this pattern, the first three plants are related to sp2 carbon and the last plant is for sp3 carbon (Baker and Baker, 2010; Chunduri et al., 2016; Tian et al., 2009). Also, no extra peak was present that indicate there were no impurities in the N-CQD.

FT-IR spectra were investigated to further study the structure of N-CQD and NiCo-LDH samples (at different molar ratio) by identifying functional groups and interlayer anions. As shown Fig. 2(a), (b), (c) and (d), at NiCo-LDH samples [Ni: Co = (1: 1), (2: 1), (3: 1) and (4: 1)], the FT-IR spectra are similar. In these spectra, the strong broadband absorption band from 3100 to 3600 cm−1 is related to the vibrations of the hydroxyl groups at the surface and interlayer water molecules (Xu et al., 2018). The band which appears in the 1638 cm−1 corresponds to the vibrational model ν1 of the interlayer water molecules (Nejati et al., 2018). The peak in the 1373 cm−1 corresponds to the O–C–O stretching vibrations of the carbonate group and confirms the presence of carbonate ions in the interlayer of prepared NiCo-LDH (Shafigh et al., 2019). The peaks in the wavelength below 830 cm−1 are due to the metal‑oxygen vibrations. Also, the FT-IR spectrum of N-CQD is presented at Fig. 2(e). A broad peak appearing at 3402 cm−1 is attributed to the OH bond. The symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate appears in the range of 1306 to 1650 cm−1 (Martindale et al., 2015). In addition, the bending vibration of CN and NH stretching vibrations appear in the 1402 and 2922 cm−1 respectively (Sun et al., 2020). The results of this spectrum confirm the synthesis of CQD containing nitrogen (N-CQD). CC vibrations appear in the region of 1604 cm−1. Also, the band vibrations at 1340 cm−1 are due to CC (Liang et al., 2013).

The size and surface morphology of the prepared NiCo-LDH samples (at various molar ratios) and N-CQD were presented at Fig. 3 (a-h) and Fig. 4 . As shown at Fig. 3(a), (b), (c), and (d), when the molar ratio Ni: Co are 1: 1 and 1: 2, spheres with the surface composed of needle-like are formed. In NiCo-LDH (Ni: Co = 1: 1), the diameter of needles is about 18 nm. This growth of nano needles was still continued at NiCo-LDH (Ni: Co = 2: 1), the diameter of needles is about 25 nm. This indicates that at NiCo-LDH with molar ratio Ni: Co = 2: 1, the size is slightly larger than that molar ratio Ni: Co = 1: 1. As the molar ratio of NiCo-LDH was increased up to 3: 1, the spheres with the surface composed of nanoplates are generated as seen in the SEM image of Fig. 3(e) and (f). This indicates that the difference in the molar ratio of Ni: Co is a key role in the formation of morphology and growth of NiCo-LDH nanostructure. As the molar ratio Ni: Co increased to 4: 1, these nanoplates are further grown as seen in the SEM image [Fig. 3(g) and (h)]. The size of nanoplates which coated the surface of spheres are about 50 nm. These hierarchical nanostructures of NiCo-LDH can effect on water oxidation and can provide a more active surface area for effective electrochemistry reaction.
Also, the surface morphology and size of the N-CQD sample was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM image (Fig. 4) reveals that the N-CQD displayed a thin.
sheet-like nanostructure with uniform and homogeneous surface morphology and lateral size of 4–8 nm.

The purity of the products was verified by EDAX spectra and the results for the NiCo-LDH (Ni: Co = 1: 1) and N-CQD are presented in Fig. 5(a) and (b). It is obvious that the products have sensible purity and all of the peaks are attributed to elements of the prepared samples. The atomic percent of Ni, O, Co, and C elements in the NiCo-LDH are 6.34, 58.13, 6.51, and 16.18, respectively [Fig. 5(a)]. Also, as shown at Fig. 5(b) the atomic percent of C, N, and O at N-CQD are 64%, 11%, and 20.7%. However, very small impurity about 3.90% (Mg: 0.44%, Ca: 0.5%, Na: 0.55%, S: 0.65%, Si: 0.47%, Cl: 0.49% and K: 0.79%) were observed at N-CQD. Also, the amount of metal ion impurity was very low (>1%) and cannot effect on the performance of N-CQD and NiCo-LDH. Furthermore, EDAX mapping images were employed to show the coexist elements in the NiCo-LDH (Ni: Co = 1: 1) and N-CQD. The images are present in Figs. 6 and 7
respectively. It is evident that coexist of the Ni, Co, O and C elements at NiCo-LDH was confirmed. Also, as shown at Fig. 7 there are homogeneous and uniformly distribution for the N, O and C elements at N-CQD.

The absorption (UV–Vis) and emission (PL) spectra of N-CQD are shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b). As shown in Fig. 8(a), a strong peak is observed at the region of 240 nm corresponding to the p–p* transitions at CC bonds due to the optical absorption in the UV–Visible curve (Naik et al., 2017). Also, a weak peak appears at 320 nm that relate to n–p* transition of CO groups at N-CQD (Liu et al., 2014). When the N-CQD was excited at excitation wavelength 235 nm, a peak at 407 nm has appeared at the emission spectrum [Fig. 8(b)]. This peak verified the blue luminescence when the appearance peak centered at 407 nm. It should be mentioned that the blue luminescence originated from the properties of prepared N-CQD. Besides, it was found that the photoluminescence spectra of N-CQDs have pertained on the size heterogeneity and excitation wavelength (Dai et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD for the water oxidation reaction
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the electro-catalytic activity of the N-CQD/NiCo-LDH for water oxidation, the samples were coated on glassy carbon electrodes for recording oxygen evaluation curves in sodium phosphate media (0.1 M, pH = 7). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were applied to investigate the applicability of the prepared products as the active material at water oxidation. Firstly, in order to optimize their water oxidation performance, LSV tests on synthesized NiCo-LDH samples under different Ni: Co molar ratios (from 1: 1 to 4: 1), Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 were investigated. Then the optimized NiCo-LDH mixed with N-CQD in order to investigate the electrochemical properties of N-CQD/NiCo-LDH. Fig. 9(a) show LSV curves of Ni(OH)2, Co(OH)2, and NiCo-LDH with different molar ratios (Ni: Co = 1: 1, 2:1, 3:1, and 4:1). It reveals that NiCo-LDH (1: 1) had a better performance in compared with Ni(OH)2, Co(OH)2, and the other molar ratios of NiCo-LDH. The results show that NiCo-LDH with the ratio of 1: 1 originates an onset potential about 1.64 V (vs. RHE) with the overpotential of 405 mV and had the better performance compared with the other molar ratios. The better water oxidation activity of this product can be due to needle-like morphology and its small size as presented by the FE-SEM graphs in Fig. 3c and d. Also, the onset potential at Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 are 1.68 and 1.75 V (vs. RHE). This confirms that the cobalt and nickel are the active electro-catalytic site (Jiang et al., 2015; Wei et al., 2019), and the combination of cobalt and nickel improves oxygen evaluation performance at optimized NiCo-LDH (1: 1). The overpotential of different NiCo-LDH are presented in Fig. 9(b). The NiCo-LDH with molar ratio (1: 1) has the lowest overpotential of 405 mV. These data confirm that the water oxidation activity of NiCo-LDH is dependent to the ratio of Ni/Co, and the optimized molar ratio in this study is 1: 1. As shown at Fig. 9(c) and (d), to evaluate the electrochemical activity of N-CQD/NiCo-LDH, the 0.01 g NiCo-LDH (1: 1) was combined with different weights of N-CQD. Based on the optimized amount of N-CQD, the N-CQD/NiCo-LDH with weight ration LDH: N-CQD = 1: 0.25, exhibits the lowest onset potential [~ 1.55 V vs (RHE)] and overpotential (325 mV) compared with those of the weight ratios of NiCo-LDH: N-CQD = (2: 1), (1: 1), (1: 2), pure N-CQD and pure NiCo-LDH (1: 1). Also, CQD/NiCo-LDH (1: 0.25) nanocomposite only required 570 mV overpotential for achieving 10 mAcm−2 current density while, about NiCo-LDH (1: 1), it needed 600 mV overpotential to reach 10 mAcm−2. So, the results construe that N-CQDs play an important role in the dramatic improvement at oxygen evaluation performance of N-CQD/NiCo-LDH due to favorable charge transfer (Zhou et al., 2014). Of course, the important note is that the pure NiCo-LDH (1: 1) also, shows a relatively high oxygen generation activity. Fig. 9(c) proposes that the N-CQD/NiCo-LDH (1: 0.25) was an improved electro-catalyst with high oxygen generation activity in neutral media. Also, based on a comparison of N-CQD and N-CQD/NiCo-LDH LSV curves at Fig. 9(c), it is deduced that strong water oxidation activity is mainly attributed to the NiCo-LDH phase. Association of the NiCo-LDH with N-CQD improved catalyst performance due to facility charge transport. Besides, as present at Fig. 9(c) and (d), the LSV results show that the pure NiCo-LDH (1: 1) and the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD with weight ratio (1: 0.5) have the overpotential of 405 mV. However, the current density of NiCo-LDH/N-CQD is lower than that pure NiCo-LDH. Also, the overpotential for NiCo-LDH/N-CQD (1: 0.5) is 608 mV which is lower than that NiCo-LDH (1: 1).

Also, for more confirm the aforesaid results and investigation of charge transfer at the present electrocatalyst, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured. Fig. 10 presents Nyquist plots of the NiCo-LDH, N-CQD, and NiCo-LDH/N-CQD (1: 0.25) composite electrodes. In all products, the plots in the high frequency range contain an almost semicircle (inset in Fig. 10) and a straight line in the region of low frequency. In general, the semicircle ascribes to the charge-transfer resistance at the interface of the electrode, while the straight line mainly illustrates solution resistance(Bao et al., 2018). Obviously as shown at Fig. 10, the semicircle of the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD (1: 0.25) in the high frequency region is lower than that of NiCo-LDH and N-CQD. This confirms a lower charge-transfer resistance at the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD (1: 0.25). Also, the slope of the line for NiCo-LDH/N-CQD is greater than that of NiCo-LDH and N-CQD, that show smaller diffusion resistance of ions in the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD. Thus, NiCo-LDH/N-CQD has a smaller charge transfer resistance and ion diffusion resistance than pure NiCo-LDH and N-CQD. These data further illustrate that the addition of N-CQD into the NiCo-LDH effect in enhanced conductivity and higher reactivity, at prepared composite and, consequently improved the water oxidation.

In subsequent studies, the stability of the optimized mixture of N-CQD and NiCo-LDH was also investigated in neutral. Fig. 11(a), can be seen that Chronoamperometric curve of NiCo-LDH/N-CQD shows relative constant current in 0.1 M sodium phosphate buffer (pH = 7). The NiCo-LDH/ N-CQD, at a constant potential [1.75 V(vs. RHE)] shows notably steady current density with 97% retention after 24 h water oxidation. Also, after 12 h Chronoamperometric, the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD has a similar LSV curve one with onset potential 1.55 V (vs. RHE) [Fig. 11(b)]. So, the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD is stable in a long-term oxygen evaluation process. As shown at Fig. 11(b), after 500 cycles of potential sweeps at 1.45–1.85 V(vs. RHE), the current density retains similar to the initial current density. These data demonstrate that NiCo-LDH/N-CQD is durable during electrocatalytic activity.

The Tafel slope introduces as a parameter to understand the kinetics of oxygen evaluation reaction. The Tafel plots of optimized NiCo-LDH/N-CQD and NiCo-LDH (1: 1) are presented in Fig. 12 . The Tafel slope for optimized NiCo-LDH/N-CQD is about 138 mV/dec that is smaller than pure NiCo-LDH (162 mV/dec) and shows the desirable oxygen evaluation kinetics of NiCo-LDH/N-CQD.

Based on the data above, the high electro-catalytic activity was due to NiCo-LDH and further improved attributed to the association of the NiCo-LDH with N-CQDs. N-CQDs supply rapid electron transfer, supreme conductivity, and electron reservoir characters. The small size of N-CQDs may enable a large specific surface area for more convenient electro-catalytic activity, and caused to the rapid electron transfers from N-CQDs to NiCo-LDH on the surface that could further increased the electro-catalytic reaction. Also, the improved water oxidation can be attributed to the synergistic effect of N-CQD and NiCo-LDH that make by direct integration and electrostatic interactions between N-CQD (CO functional groups upon the surface) and NiCo-LDH. Therefore, the combination of N-CQD and NiCo-LDH as excellent electro-catalysts suggests a new approach for high efficiency electro-catalyst preparation and provides a pathway to green chemistry and the use of low-cost materials such as weeds in the generation of energy.

In summary, we report the synthesis of N-CQD using field bindweed and hydrothermal method. Then the NiCo-LDH prepared using a simple method. The prepared N-CQD and NiCo-LDH have been characterized by several techniques to study their structure. The results show that the combination of NiCo-LDH (molar ratio Ni: Co =1: 1) and N-CQD shows improved electro-catalyst character for water oxidation. This easy and inexpensively prepared electro-catalyst, shows excellent oxygen evaluation with a small onset-potential of 1.55 V and overpotential about 325 mV in 0.1 M sodium buffer solution (pH = 7). Base on Nyquist plots, the key advantages of the NiCo-LDH/N-CQD, include rapid electron transfer and supreme conductivity due to the presence of the N-CQD for more convenient electro-catalytic activity. Also, the improved water oxidation can attributed to the synergistic effect of N-CQD and NiCo-LDH that make by direct integration and electrostatic interactions between N-CQD (CO functional groups upon the surface) and NiCo-LDH. Besides, the chronopotentiometry results presented the proper durability of NiCo-LDH/N-CQD. Our researches supply a new insight into the design and fabrication of effective oxygen evaluation electro-catalysts by synergistic of NiCo-LDH and N-CQDs, which are forcefully needed for storage technologies and energy conversion.

Leila Jafari Foruzin: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Conceptualization, Visualization, Project administration. Zolfaghar Rezvani: Supervision, Resources, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) and Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University under grant number 97018602.
